blurb: Why the action always hides at the end

sections.heading: The verb hides at the end
body: The Uzbek verb is shy: it always hides at the **end of the sentence**. The pattern is Subject – Object – **Verb**.
**Men non yedim** is literally “I bread ate”. It feels strange for a day or two — then your brain flips, and English starts sounding backwards.

sections.heading: Everything points the same way
body: Uzbek is consistently **head-final**: describing things come before the thing described. Adjectives before nouns (**katta shahar** — big city), owners before what they own, and the little relation words come **after** the noun: **men bilan** — “me with”.
One more tip: the word just before the verb carries the spotlight. But verb-last is always the safe default.

exercises.instruction: Which one sounds natural to an Uzbek ear?

options.explain: The verb has wandered into the middle — it belongs at the end.
The verb goes last, not first.

wrapUp: Subject, then object, then — at the very end — the verb. Park the action at the end of the sentence and you'll sound right.
